1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
71	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
72	associative.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Affine base change]
76	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
78	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
79	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
80	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
81	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
82	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
83	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
84	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
85	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
86	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
87	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
88	  % we have
89	  % $$
90	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
91	  % $$"
92	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
93	  change''.}
94	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
95	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
96	  above the canonical map of
97	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
100	  \]
101	\end{lemma}
102	
103	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
104	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
105	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
106	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
107	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
108	% for some $A$-module $M$.
109	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
110	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
111	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
112	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
113	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
114	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
115	% equality
116	% $$
117	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
118	% $$
119	% as $R'$-modules."
120	% NOTE: Lean proof incomplete (sorry at the section-iso step). The
121	% `Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app` reduction lands, but closing the per-section goal
122	% needs a Mathlib gap NOT yet present: the affine dictionary translating
123	% Scheme.Modules.pushforward/pullback of tilde-modules on Spec into
124	% restrictScalars / base change, plus an "iso local on an affine cover"
125	% criterion for SheafOfModules maps. The closing algebra
126	% (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange) exists in Mathlib. See
127	% informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
130	  The assertion is local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all schemes in
131	  sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
132	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\) corresponding to a ring map
133	  \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the quasi-coherent sheaf
134	  associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). Computing the fibre product gives
135	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
136	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
137	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). Under the
138	  dictionary between quasi-coherent sheaves on an affine scheme and modules,
139	  pushforward along an affine morphism is restriction of scalars and pullback
140	  along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_R R'\). The base-change map of
141	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is therefore identified with
142	  the canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison map for the two ways of forming the
143	  iterated tensor product, and the claim reduces to the associativity isomorphism
144	  \[
145	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;=\; R' \otimes_R M
146	  \]
147	  of \(R'\)-modules, which is an isomorphism (no flatness is needed in the affine
148	  case).
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
152	
153	\begin{theorem}\leanok
154	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
155	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
157	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
158	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
159	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
160	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
161	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
162	  % $$
163	  % \xymatrix{
164	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
165	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
166	  % }
167	  % $$
168	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
169	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
170	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
171	  % For any $i \geq 0$
172	  % \begin{enumerate}
173	  % \item the base change map of
174	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
175	  % is an isomorphism
176	  % $$
177	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
178	  % $$
179	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
180	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
181	  % \end{enumerate}"
182	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
183	  \(i = 0\) case.}
184	  Consider the cartesian square
185	  \[
186	    \begin{array}{ccc}
187	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
188	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
189	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
190	    \end{array}
191	  \]
192	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
193	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
194	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
195	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
196	  \[
197	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
198	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
199	  \]
200	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
201	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
202	  \[
203	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
204	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
205	  \]
206	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
207	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
208	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
209	\end{theorem}
210	
211	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
212	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
213	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
214	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
215	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
216	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
217	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
218	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
219	% (equality by
220	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
221	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
222	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
223	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
224	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
225	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
226	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
227	%
228	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
229	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
230	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
231	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
232	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
233	% We are trying to show that the map
234	% $$
235	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
236	% $$
237	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
238	% is an isomorphism.
239	%
240	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
241	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
242	% $$
243	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
244	% $$
245	% see
246	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
247	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
248	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
249	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
250	% $$
251	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
252	% $$
253	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
254	% $$
255	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
256	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
257	% $$
258	% as follows from
259	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
260	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
261	% NOTE: Lean proof is a documented sorry (deferred). Depends on the affine
262	% lemma above PLUS Čech-cohomology / affine-cover infrastructure for
263	% SheafOfModules, which Mathlib does not provide. This is a multi-lane engine
264	% build, not a single-iter target. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
267	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
268	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
269	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
270	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
271	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
272	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
273	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
274	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
275	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
276	  \(B\)-modules
277	  \[
278	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
279	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
280	  \]
281	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
282	
283	  \medskip
284	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
285	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
286	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
287	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
288	  computes sheaf cohomology:
289	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
290	  the cover yields an affine open cover
291	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
292	  \(X_B\), with the same property
293	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
294	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
295	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
296	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
297	  \[
298	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
299	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
300	  \]
301	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
302	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
303	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
304	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
305	
306	  \medskip
307	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
308	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
309	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
310	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
311	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
312	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
313	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
314	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
315	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
316	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
317	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
318	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
319	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
320	  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
321	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
322	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
323	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
324	  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
325	\end{proof}

4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
10	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
11	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
12	    multi-route coverage.
13	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
14	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
15	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
16	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
17	    what the directive lists.
18	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
19	    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
20	    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
21	    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
22	    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review

24	    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
25	    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
26	    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
28	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the

53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
177	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
178	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
179	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
180	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
181	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
182	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
183	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
184	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
185	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
186	
187	## Reading references
188	
189	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
190	
191	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
192	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
193	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
194	
195	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
196	
197	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
198	
199	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
200	
201	Conditions for dispatch:
202	
203	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
204	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
205	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
206	
207	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
208	
209	```
210	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
211	  --name reference-retriever \
212	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
213	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
214	  --write-domain 'references/**'
215	```
216	
217	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
218	
219	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
220	
221	## Workflow
222	
223	1. Read your directive completely.
224	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
225	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
226	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
227	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
228	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
229	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
230	8. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
231

\section{The affine case}

\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}

Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
citation and stand on their proof sketches.

\begin{lemma}[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
  isomorphism.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
  affine opens.
\end{proof}

\subsection{The affine computation}

The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
associative.
